description: Kolatron.ai v1 Manufacturing Diagnostic Knowledge Base — 30 common faults across packaging, conveyors, pneumatics, sensors, motors, VFDs, PLCs, safety circuits, and production equipment.

faults.title: Conveyor Belt Tracking Failure
symptoms: Belt drifting to one side during operation
Belt edge contact with conveyor frame
Belt edge fraying or tearing
Product spillage or misalignment on belt
Unusual squealing or rubbing noise from belt edges
faults.meaning: The conveyor belt is not running centrally on its carrying and return idlers. Left uncorrected, belt edge damage accelerates rapidly, and in severe cases the belt can detrack completely, causing a production stoppage and potential injury risk from a loose moving belt.

causes.cause: Tail or head pulley misalignment — pulleys not square to the conveyor frame centreline
Uneven belt tension across the width due to off-centre loading or splice misalignment
Idler rollers seized, worn, or installed at incorrect angle
Product loading consistently off-centre causing lateral belt loading
Belt splice incorrectly made — not square to belt centreline
Conveyor frame twisted or structurally distorted
Tracking checks not included in planned maintenance schedule

checks: Isolate and lock out the conveyor before any physical inspection of pulleys, idlers, or belt edges
With conveyor stopped, check head and tail pulley alignment using a straight edge or laser alignment tool — pulleys must be square to the frame centreline
Inspect all carrying and return idlers for seized bearings or incorrect installation angle
Check belt splice for squareness — a skewed splice will cause systematic tracking drift
Observe belt tracking with conveyor running at normal speed from a safe distance — note which direction drift occurs and at which point on the conveyor it begins
Check product loading point — confirm product is centred on the belt
Inspect belt edges for wear, fraying, or previous tracking damage
safety_precautions: Full isolation and lockout/tagout (LOTO) required before any hands-on inspection of belt, pulleys, or idlers — in line with safe isolation principles and PUWER guidance
Never attempt to push or guide a moving belt by hand
Beware of nip points between belt and pulleys — these are entrapment hazards
If belt has detracked significantly, do not restart until belt is repositioned and cause is identified
Where belt edge is contacting the frame, there is a risk of fire from friction — check for heat before touching
faults.escalation: Escalate to engineering if pulley alignment adjustment does not resolve tracking within two adjustment attempts, if the conveyor frame is found to be twisted or damaged, or if the belt splice is misaligned and a re-splice is required.
faults.documentation_notes: Record the direction of drift, the point at which drift begins, any pulley adjustments made (including before and after measurements), and the belt condition at the time of the fault. If in a GMP environment, raise a maintenance work order and log as a controlled maintenance activity.
faults.gmp_notes: In food, pharmaceutical, or medical device manufacturing, belt edge damage creates a foreign body contamination risk. If belt fraying is observed, assess whether any belt material may have entered the product stream and notify QA. A controlled change may be required if pulley alignment settings are formally documented in equipment qualification records.
preventive_actions: Include belt tracking check in weekly or monthly planned maintenance schedule
Check and record pulley alignment at each belt change
Inspect belt splice condition at each planned maintenance visit
Confirm product loading is centred — adjust chutes or transfer guides as needed
reliability_improvements: Install self-tracking idlers at key tracking zones if persistent drift is a recurring issue
Fit belt tracking sensors with alarm output to PLC to give early warning before belt edge damage occurs
Consider laser alignment verification of pulleys at each annual overhaul

faults.title: Conveyor Belt Slip on Drive Pulley
symptoms: Belt slipping audibly on drive pulley under load
Belt speed reducing under load while drive motor continues at speed
Burning rubber smell from drive pulley area
Drive pulley lagging worn smooth or glazed
Belt tension insufficient — belt visibly slack on return run
faults.meaning: The belt is losing traction on the drive pulley. The pulley is rotating but not transmitting sufficient drive force to the belt. Continued slip will glaze both the belt underside and pulley lagging, making the condition worse and risking belt and lagging damage.

causes.cause: Insufficient belt tension — take-up not adjusted or counterweight insufficient
Drive pulley lagging worn, glazed, or contaminated with oil or grease
Conveyor overloaded beyond design capacity
Belt underside surface glazed from previous slip episodes
Take-up mechanism seized or not functioning
Lagging replacement not scheduled in planned maintenance

checks: Isolate and lock out before inspecting drive pulley lagging condition
Inspect drive pulley lagging — check for wear, glazing, cracking, or contamination
Check belt tension at the take-up — adjust to manufacturer specification
Check take-up mechanism for free movement — gravity take-ups must be free to travel, screw take-ups must not be at end of travel
Inspect belt underside at drive pulley contact zone for glazing
Check conveyor loading — confirm it is within design limits
Check for oil or grease contamination at the drive pulley from bearing or gearbox leaks
safety_precautions: Isolation and LOTO required before any inspection of the drive pulley or lagging
Burning rubber from slip is a fire risk — check for smouldering material before work begins
Beware stored energy in gravity take-up counterweights — do not work under an unsupported counterweight
If belt has slipped significantly off the pulley, do not attempt to reposition by hand while power is available
faults.escalation: Escalate if lagging replacement is required — this typically requires belt removal and a planned shutdown. Escalate if take-up travel is exhausted and the belt requires re-tensioning or re-splicing.
faults.documentation_notes: Record take-up adjustment made, lagging condition found, and loading at time of fault. Note any contamination source identified.
faults.gmp_notes: Burning rubber or lagging debris is a contamination risk in food and pharmaceutical environments. If slip has occurred near an open product zone, notify QA and assess contamination risk.
preventive_actions: Include drive pulley lagging inspection in planned maintenance schedule
Check belt tension and take-up position at each planned maintenance visit
Check for gearbox or bearing oil leaks at drive end at each inspection
reliability_improvements: Fit ceramic or high-friction lagging on drive pulley in high-load or wet applications
Install belt slip detection (speed comparison between motor and belt) with PLC alarm

faults.title: Conveyor Drive Motor Overheating
symptoms: Motor thermal protection tripping
Motor casing hot to touch
Motor running at reduced speed or stalling under load
Burning smell from motor
Motor trip on overload relay
faults.meaning: The conveyor drive motor is exceeding its thermal rating. Repeated thermal trips without identifying and correcting the cause will degrade motor winding insulation and shorten motor life significantly. In worst cases, winding failure occurs.

causes.cause: Conveyor mechanically overloaded — product buildup, seized idler, or belt tension too high
Motor cooling fan obstructed or fan cowl damaged
Gearbox fault — bearing failure or oil loss causing increased mechanical resistance
Overload relay set incorrectly — set too high allowing motor to overheat before tripping
Motor operating in high ambient temperature environment without adequate ventilation
Motor nearing end of life — winding insulation degraded

checks: Allow motor to cool before inspection — do not touch immediately after a thermal trip
Isolate and lock out before inspecting mechanical drive train
Check motor cooling fan and fan cowl — ensure fan is intact and air inlet is clear
Check gearbox for unusual noise, temperature, or oil leaks
Manually rotate the drive train (with power isolated) to check for mechanical resistance or seized components
Check overload relay setting against motor nameplate full load current
Check conveyor loading at time of fault
Check ambient temperature around motor
safety_precautions: Do not touch motor casing immediately after thermal trip — surface may be hot enough to cause burns
Isolation and LOTO required before any inspection of the mechanical drive train
Confirm motor has cooled sufficiently before restart — allow at least 20-30 minutes after a thermal trip
Do not override or bypass the overload relay to force a restart without identifying the cause
faults.escalation: Escalate if motor winding insulation resistance test (megger test) shows degraded insulation. Escalate if gearbox bearing failure is found. Escalate if motor requires rewinding or replacement.
faults.documentation_notes: Record motor running current at time of fault if measurable. Record overload relay setting. Record ambient temperature. Note number of thermal trips in preceding period.
faults.gmp_notes: In GMP environments, motor replacement or significant repair may require re-qualification or documented change control, particularly if the motor drives process-critical equipment.
preventive_actions: Check motor cooling fan and cowl condition at each planned maintenance visit
Verify overload relay setting against motor nameplate at commissioning and after any motor change
Monitor motor running current periodically and trend against baseline
reliability_improvements: Install motor current monitoring with trending to detect gradual load increases before thermal trip occurs
Consider motor with built-in PTC thermistor for more precise thermal protection

faults.title: Pneumatic Cylinder Failure to Extend or Retract
symptoms: Cylinder rod not moving on command signal
Cylinder moving slowly or with reduced force
Cylinder stalling mid-stroke
PLC input from cylinder position sensor not changing state
Audible air exhaust from cylinder port without movement
faults.meaning: The pneumatic cylinder is not completing its commanded stroke. The fault may be in the air supply, solenoid valve, internal cylinder seals, mechanical obstruction, or control signal. Multiple factors can combine — systematic elimination is required.

causes.cause: Solenoid valve not energising or not shifting — coil fault, valve spool stuck, or no control signal
Insufficient air pressure at the cylinder — regulator set low, supply issue, or filter blocked
Internal piston seal failure — air bypassing piston without generating force
Mechanical obstruction preventing cylinder stroke
Flow control restrictor set too tight on exhaust port — cylinder starved of exhaust flow
Cylinder rod seal failure — air escaping past rod seal
Cylinder bore corroded or damaged — piston binding

checks: Check air supply pressure at the cylinder — compare to specified working pressure
Check filter/regulator unit upstream — drain water trap if needed
Confirm solenoid valve is receiving the correct control signal from the PLC
Manually actuate the solenoid valve (if fitted with manual override) with machine in safe state
Check for air escaping from cylinder rod seal or exhaust ports when valve is energised
Check flow control restrictors — ensure they are not fully closed
Inspect cylinder rod and bore for mechanical obstruction or damage
Check cylinder mounting — misalignment causes side loading and piston binding
safety_precautions: Before any work on the cylinder or valve, isolate and vent the pneumatic supply — stored pressure in cylinders is a serious hazard
Use only the designated isolation valve for the circuit — confirm pressure has dropped to zero before disconnecting any fitting
Cylinder rods under spring return or gravity load can move unexpectedly when pressure is removed — support any load before venting
Manual override of solenoid valves should only be used with the machine in a safe state and all personnel clear of the cylinder stroke zone
faults.escalation: Escalate if cylinder requires internal seal replacement — requires cylinder removal and strip. Escalate if solenoid valve coil or spool requires replacement. Escalate if air supply pressure cannot be restored to specification.
faults.documentation_notes: Record working pressure found, solenoid signal status, and the specific fault mode. Record any air leaks found and their location.
faults.gmp_notes: Pneumatic cylinder lubricants should use food-grade lubricant where there is any risk of contact with product or packaging. Cylinder seal replacement should be documented.
preventive_actions: Include filter/regulator drain and element inspection in weekly planned maintenance
Check cylinder rod seal condition and rod surface for corrosion at each planned maintenance visit
Verify solenoid valve operation at each planned maintenance visit
reliability_improvements: Install pressure monitoring with low pressure alarm before cylinder circuits
Consider guided cylinders in applications with significant side loading to reduce seal and bore wear

faults.title: Solenoid Valve Not Shifting
symptoms: Cylinder or actuator not responding to command
PLC output shown as energised but actuator not moving
Solenoid coil warm or hot without valve shifting
Audible click from solenoid but no valve movement
Air leaking from valve exhaust port continuously
faults.meaning: The solenoid valve is either not receiving a signal, the coil is not generating sufficient magnetic force, or the valve spool is mechanically stuck. Commonly caused by contamination, coil failure, or voltage issues.

causes.cause: Solenoid coil burned out — coil open circuit, no magnetic field generated
Valve spool contaminated or stuck — particulate in air supply or sticky residue on spool
Incorrect voltage at solenoid connector — wiring fault, loose connection, or PLC output fault
Voltage too low at coil — voltage drop due to long cable run or poor connection
Contaminated or wet air supply causing spool corrosion or seal swelling
Wrong voltage coil fitted during previous replacement

checks: Confirm PLC output is energised — check PLC status monitor, not just the indicator lamp
Measure voltage at the solenoid coil connector with a multimeter — confirm correct voltage present
Check coil resistance with multimeter (power off) — open circuit or very low resistance indicates coil fault
Attempt manual override (if fitted) with machine in safe state — if valve shifts manually but not electrically, confirms coil or signal fault
If valve does not shift on manual override, suspect spool contamination or mechanical fault
Check air supply for contamination — inspect filter bowl for water, oil, or particles
Check coil voltage rating matches supply voltage
safety_precautions: Isolate and vent pneumatic supply before removing or replacing solenoid valves
Confirm voltage is isolated before disconnecting solenoid connectors
Manual override must only be used with all personnel clear of actuator stroke zone
Hot solenoid coils indicate sustained energisation without shifting — do not touch without checking temperature first
faults.escalation: Escalate if valve body requires cleaning or rebuilding. Escalate if PLC output module is suspected. Escalate if contamination source in air supply cannot be identified.
faults.documentation_notes: Record coil resistance measured, voltage found at connector, and whether manual override confirmed mechanical or electrical fault. Record replacement part number if coil or valve replaced.
faults.gmp_notes: Valve replacement in GMP environments should be documented. Confirm replacement valve material specification is appropriate for the application.
preventive_actions: Check air quality at filter/regulator regularly — drain water traps and replace filter elements on schedule
Inspect solenoid connectors for corrosion or loose terminations at each planned maintenance visit
Keep coil replacement spares on site for all solenoid valve types in use
reliability_improvements: Fit air quality monitoring on main compressed air supply
Use sealed solenoid connectors in wet or washdown environments

faults.title: Low Air Pressure Alarm
symptoms: Low air pressure alarm on HMI or PLC
Machine stopping or running in degraded mode
Cylinders moving slowly or with reduced force
Pressure gauge reading below set threshold
Air leaks audible from pipework, fittings, or equipment
faults.meaning: The compressed air pressure in the machine circuit has fallen below the minimum required for safe and reliable operation. Operating below minimum pressure causes cylinders to generate insufficient force, sensors to malfunction, and safety functions that rely on air pressure to become unreliable.

causes.cause: Air leak in the machine circuit — fittings, push-in connectors, hoses, or cylinder seals
Compressed air supply pressure low — compressor fault, demand exceeding supply, or main line issue
Filter/regulator element blocked — restricting flow
Pressure regulator set incorrectly or regulator fault
Multiple machines simultaneously increasing demand beyond supply capacity
Air receiver tank drained or not refilling

checks: Check supply pressure at the main machine inlet — compare to site supply specification
Check machine regulator setting and output pressure
Check filter element — replace if blocked
Walk the machine air circuit listening and feeling for air leaks — check all push-in fittings, hose connections, and cylinder rod seals
Check whether low pressure is site-wide or machine-specific
Check compressor status if site-wide low pressure is suspected
safety_precautions: Do not attempt to locate leaks by feel alone near high-pressure fittings — air injection injury is a serious medical risk
Use leak detection spray or ultrasonic leak detector for finding leaks safely
Do not raise supply pressure above machine design rating to compensate for leaks
If machine has safety functions that depend on air pressure, confirm these are functional before restart
faults.escalation: Escalate if compressor fault is identified. Escalate if main air receiver or main supply pipework is leaking. Escalate if pressure cannot be restored within safe operating parameters.
faults.documentation_notes: Record supply pressure at fault, regulator output pressure, and location of any leaks found and repaired. Record filter element condition.
faults.gmp_notes: In GMP environments, compressed air quality (oil content, dew point, particulate level) should meet the specification for the application.
preventive_actions: Include air leak survey in monthly planned maintenance schedule
Replace filter elements on a time-based or pressure differential schedule
Check and record machine inlet pressure at each planned maintenance visit
reliability_improvements: Install pressure monitoring with data logging to trend pressure over time
Conduct formal compressed air leak survey using ultrasonic detector on an annual basis

faults.title: Vacuum Pick Head Failing to Pick Product
symptoms: Product not being picked up by vacuum head
Product dropping mid-transfer
Vacuum level gauge or sensor reading below required level
Suction cup visibly not seating on product surface
Vacuum pump or ejector running continuously without achieving setpoint
faults.meaning: The vacuum pick system is not generating sufficient vacuum at the suction cup face to hold the product. This can be caused by leakage in the vacuum circuit, suction cup wear or damage, product surface variation, or a fault in the vacuum generation equipment.

causes.cause: Suction cup worn, cracked, or hardened — no longer sealing against product surface
Vacuum circuit leak — fitting, hose, or valve not sealing
Product surface variation — wrinkled, wet, porous, or uneven surface breaking vacuum seal
Venturi ejector or vacuum pump performance degraded — filter blocked or ejector nozzle worn
Suction cup not making contact with product — pick height or position incorrect
Vacuum solenoid valve not fully opening — partial flow restriction

checks: Inspect suction cups — check for cracks, hardening, deformation, or contamination on the sealing lip
Check vacuum level at the pick head with product in contact — measure actual vacuum generated
Isolate and check vacuum circuit for leaks — listen and check fittings, hoses, and valve connections
Check venturi ejector — ensure compressed air supply to ejector is at correct pressure and ejector nozzle is not blocked
Check pick head position and stroke — confirm cup is making firm contact with product
Inspect product surface at pick zone — check for moisture, wrinkling, or porosity variations
safety_precautions: Isolate and vent the pneumatic and vacuum supply before replacing suction cups or working on the vacuum circuit
Where pick heads operate on high-speed machinery, ensure guarding is in place before any adjustment
Do not place hands in the pick zone with machine running
faults.escalation: Escalate if venturi ejector requires replacement or if vacuum pump requires service. Escalate if pick head position requires mechanical adjustment beyond operator scope.
faults.documentation_notes: Record vacuum level measured at pick head, suction cup condition found, and any leaks identified and repaired.
faults.gmp_notes: In food and pharmaceutical packaging, suction cup material must be food-grade and approved for the application. Worn suction cup material is a potential contamination source.
preventive_actions: Include suction cup inspection and replacement in planned maintenance schedule
Check vacuum circuit fittings and hoses at each planned maintenance visit
Check venturi ejector nozzle condition at each planned maintenance
reliability_improvements: Install vacuum sensor with PLC monitoring and alarm on each pick head
Consider bellows-type suction cups for applications with variable product surface profiles

faults.title: Inductive Proximity Sensor Not Detecting
symptoms: PLC input from sensor not changing state when target is present
Machine not advancing sequence — waiting for sensor signal
Sensor indicator LED not illuminating when target in range
Sensor output stuck on or stuck off
faults.meaning: The inductive proximity sensor is not detecting the metal target reliably. This can halt a machine sequence or cause incorrect sequencing. Inductive sensors detect metallic targets only — if the target is non-metallic, this is the wrong sensor type.

causes.cause: Sensor-to-target gap too large — sensor out of position or target not approaching close enough
Sensor face contaminated — metal swarf, oil, or debris reducing sensing range
Sensor physically damaged — impact damage to sensor face or body
Wiring fault — broken wire, loose connector, or incorrect wiring (NPN/PNP mismatch)
Sensor supply voltage incorrect or absent
Wrong sensor type for target material

checks: Check sensor indicator LED — most sensors have a yellow LED that illuminates when target is detected
Measure sensor-to-target gap and compare to sensor datasheet sensing range
Clean sensor face and check for contamination
Check sensor supply voltage at the connector (typically 10-30VDC)
Check wiring continuity and connector termination
Substitute with a known good sensor to confirm whether fault is sensor or wiring/PLC input
Check PLC input channel status in online monitoring
safety_precautions: Where sensor is mounted in a moving zone, ensure machine is isolated before adjusting sensor position
Confirm machine is in a safe state before reaching into guarded areas to inspect or replace sensors
Follow LOTO procedure if sensor is inside a guarded enclosure
faults.escalation: Escalate if PLC input module is suspected faulty. Escalate if sensor replacement does not resolve the fault.
faults.documentation_notes: Record sensor gap measured, contamination found, and replacement sensor part number. Note PLC input channel number.
faults.gmp_notes: In GMP environments, sensor replacement should be documented. Confirm replacement sensor specification matches the original.
preventive_actions: Include sensor face cleaning and gap check in planned maintenance schedule
Check sensor mounting security at each planned maintenance visit
reliability_improvements: Use sensors with IO-Link for remote diagnostics and condition monitoring
Protect sensors in high-contamination zones with sensor guards or flush-mount installation

faults.title: Photoelectric Sensor False Triggering or Missing Detection
symptoms: Sensor triggering without product present (false trigger)
Sensor not triggering when product is present (missed detection)
Erratic PLC input — signal flickering
Machine ejecting good product or failing to eject faulty product
Sensor indicator LED flickering or showing marginal detection
faults.meaning: The photoelectric sensor is giving unreliable detection. False triggers cause unnecessary rejects or incorrect machine actions. Missed detections cause quality failures. Both are serious in a manufacturing or packaging environment.

causes.cause: Sensor lens contaminated — dust, product residue, moisture, or condensation reducing signal margin
Sensor alignment incorrect — beam not centred on target or reflector
Ambient light interference — strong direct light entering sensor lens
Product surface variation — shiny, transparent, or dark products near sensor sensitivity limit
Reflector contaminated or damaged (retro-reflective type)
Vibration causing sensor to move out of alignment
Sensitivity potentiometer set incorrectly

checks: Clean sensor lens and reflector (if applicable) with lint-free cloth
Check sensor indicator — most sensors show a signal strength or margin indicator
Verify sensor alignment — beam should be centred on the target or reflector with good margin
Check for ambient light interference — shield sensor from direct light sources if necessary
Check sensor mounting security
Check product surface characteristics at the detection zone
Adjust sensitivity if sensor has a manual adjustment
safety_precautions: If sensor is part of a safety function (light curtain etc.), do not adjust or bypass without a formal risk assessment — follow ISO 13849 principles for safety-related systems
Standard photoelectric sensors used for product detection are typically not safety-rated — confirm before bypassing any sensor signal
Machine isolation required before accessing sensor in guarded zone
faults.escalation: Escalate if sensor type is fundamentally unsuitable for the product — a different sensor technology may be required.
faults.documentation_notes: Record contamination found, alignment adjustment made, and sensor signal margin before and after adjustment. Note product type being detected.
faults.gmp_notes: In GMP environments, sensor lens cleaning should use approved cleaning materials. If sensor is part of a rejection system, any adjustment should be validated by running a test sequence.
preventive_actions: Include sensor lens and reflector cleaning in daily or weekly planned maintenance schedule
Check sensor alignment and signal margin at each planned maintenance visit
reliability_improvements: Use sensors with IO-Link for remote signal margin monitoring
Consider laser sensors for improved margin on difficult product surfaces
Install sensor self-cleaning air purge in dusty environments

faults.title: Motor Overload Trip
symptoms: Motor overload relay tripped
Machine stopped — motor circuit fault on HMI
Motor not restarting after reset
Overload relay requiring repeated manual resets
Motor running hot before trip
faults.meaning: The motor has drawn current exceeding the overload relay setting long enough to cause a thermal trip. The overload relay is working correctly — the fault is in the mechanical system or electrical supply, not the overload relay itself.

causes.cause: Mechanical overload — product jam, seized bearing, belt too tight, or conveyor overloaded
Motor bearing failure — increased mechanical friction causing higher current draw
Supply voltage low — motor drawing higher current to maintain torque
Single-phase supply (phase loss) — three-phase motor running on two phases, overheating rapidly
Overload relay set too low — set below motor full load current
Motor winding fault — degraded insulation increasing current draw

checks: Before resetting, allow motor to cool for at least 20-30 minutes
Isolate and LOTO before mechanical inspection
Manually rotate the motor and driven load — check for unusual resistance, stiffness, or seizure
Check for product jam or mechanical obstruction
Check overload relay setting against motor nameplate full load current (FLC) — typical setting 1.0 to 1.05 x FLC
Check three-phase supply voltage at the motor starter — confirm all three phases are present and balanced
Check motor bearing condition — listen for noise, check for heat at bearing housings
Measure motor running current after restart and compare to FLC
safety_precautions: Never bypass or increase the overload relay setting as a quick fix — this removes motor protection and creates fire and winding damage risk
Isolation and LOTO required before any mechanical inspection
Phase loss can cause rapid motor damage — do not restart until supply is confirmed balanced
Allow motor to cool fully before restart
faults.escalation: Escalate if phase loss is identified — requires electrical engineer. Escalate if motor winding insulation test shows degradation. Escalate if motor bearing replacement is required.
faults.documentation_notes: Record overload relay setting, motor FLC from nameplate, running current if measured, and mechanical condition found.
faults.gmp_notes: Motor replacement in GMP environments may require equipment re-qualification. Confirm replacement motor specification matches original.
preventive_actions: Include motor bearing inspection and running current check in planned maintenance schedule
Verify overload relay setting after any motor or starter replacement
Check for product jam risk zones and implement mechanical jam prevention where possible
reliability_improvements: Install motor current monitoring with trending to detect gradual load increase before trip
Consider motor protection relay with phase loss detection and data logging for critical motors

faults.title: VFD Fault Trip
symptoms: VFD showing fault code on display
Motor controlled by VFD has stopped
VFD fault relay output active
VFD display showing overcurrent, overvoltage, undervoltage, or overtemperature fault code
VFD not accepting run command after fault reset
faults.meaning: The VFD has detected a condition outside its operating limits and has tripped to protect itself and the motor. The fault code on the VFD display is the starting point for diagnosis — each fault type has a different root cause path.

causes.cause: Overcurrent trip — mechanical overload, rapid acceleration, or motor fault causing excess current
Overtemperature trip — VFD cooling fan failed, inlet blocked, or ambient temperature too high
Overvoltage trip — regenerative braking energy not dissipated, or supply voltage spike
Undervoltage trip — supply voltage low or supply interruption
Earth fault trip — motor or cable insulation fault to earth
Communication fault — VFD not receiving command from PLC via fieldbus
VFD parameters incorrect after power loss or replacement

checks: Read and record the VFD fault code before resetting — this is critical diagnostic information
Check VFD fault history log if available
For overcurrent: check motor and mechanical load for obstruction or overload
For overtemperature: check VFD cooling fan operation, check air inlet and outlet for obstruction
For overvoltage: check if braking resistor is fitted and functional
For earth fault: perform insulation resistance test on motor windings and cable (power off and isolated from VFD)
For communication fault: check fieldbus connections and PLC communication status
Check VFD parameter settings if recently replaced or after a power loss
safety_precautions: VFD DC bus capacitors retain dangerous voltage after power isolation — wait at least 5 minutes after isolating power before opening VFD enclosure and verify voltage has discharged
Do not short-circuit or earth the DC bus to discharge capacitors
Insulation resistance testing must only be performed with VFD disconnected from the motor terminals — megger voltage will damage VFD input circuitry
Isolation and LOTO required before any work on VFD panel or motor connections
faults.escalation: Escalate if earth fault is confirmed — motor or cable replacement required. Escalate if VFD internal fault is suspected. Escalate if VFD requires replacement and parameter backup is not available.
faults.documentation_notes: Record fault code, fault history, VFD parameter settings (back up if possible). Record any mechanical findings. Note ambient temperature at VFD enclosure.
faults.gmp_notes: VFD replacement in GMP environments may require re-qualification of drive speed parameters if these are part of a validated process.
preventive_actions: Include VFD cooling fan inspection and enclosure filter cleaning in planned maintenance schedule
Back up VFD parameters after commissioning and after any parameter change
Check VFD enclosure ambient temperature at each planned maintenance visit
reliability_improvements: Install VFD with remote monitoring capability
Fit braking resistor on applications with regenerative loads
Consider VFD with built-in condition monitoring functions

faults.title: PLC CPU Fault or Stop Mode
symptoms: PLC CPU in STOP mode — RUN LED off, STOP LED on
Machine completely stopped — no outputs active
PLC ERROR LED illuminated
PLC programming software showing CPU fault or error code
Machine stopped without any machine-level fault trigger
faults.meaning: The PLC CPU has entered a fault or STOP state, halting all machine outputs. All machine motion and process control will be suspended. The cause must be identified before restarting — an unexplained CPU STOP may indicate a program fault, hardware fault, or a safety-related condition.

causes.cause: Program execution error — division by zero, illegal instruction, or array out of bounds in user program
Hardware fault — CPU module failure, memory fault, or backplane fault
Power supply fault — CPU supply voltage out of tolerance
Watchdog timeout — program scan time exceeded maximum cycle time
I/O module fault — short circuit or overload on output module causing system fault
Unauthorised or incomplete program change
Firmware corruption or version mismatch after module replacement

checks: Connect programming software to PLC and read the CPU diagnostic buffer — this contains the fault code and fault time
Record all fault codes before attempting to clear
Check CPU module LEDs — note which LEDs are on (RUN, STOP, ERROR, MAINT, LINK)
Check PLC power supply voltage output
Check I/O module status LEDs — look for module fault indicators
Review recent program changes if any were made
Check for short circuits on output module field wiring
safety_precautions: Do not attempt to force the PLC back to RUN mode without understanding why it went to STOP — this could cause uncontrolled machine movement
Before restarting after a CPU fault, confirm all outputs are in a safe state and all personnel are clear
Program changes on live production PLCs must follow a formal change control process — in line with IEC 61131 good practice
Ensure that resetting a CPU fault does not inadvertently bypass a safety function
faults.escalation: Escalate to controls engineer if program fault is identified — do not attempt to modify PLC program without authorisation. Escalate if CPU hardware replacement is required.
faults.documentation_notes: Record all CPU diagnostic buffer fault codes with timestamps. Record any recent program changes. Record CPU firmware version.
faults.gmp_notes: In GMP environments, PLC program changes require formal change control and may require revalidation. Document the fault, investigation, and corrective action.
preventive_actions: Back up PLC program and CPU configuration after any program change
Include PLC power supply voltage check in planned maintenance
Monitor CPU scan time and raise an alarm if scan time approaches watchdog limit
reliability_improvements: Implement CPU redundancy on critical lines
Use PLC remote monitoring to detect CPU faults and alert engineering team immediately

faults.title: PLC Digital Output Not Activating Field Device
symptoms: PLC output shown as active in program but field device not energising
Output indicator LED on I/O module lit but no voltage at field terminal
Solenoid valve, relay, or actuator not responding to PLC command
Output LED not lit even though program logic should be true
faults.meaning: There is a break in the control chain between the PLC program and the field device. The fault may be in the PLC output module, wiring, load, or power supply. Systematic elimination is required.

causes.cause: Output module channel blown — output transistor or triac failed due to overcurrent or short circuit
Wiring fault — broken wire or loose terminal between field device and PLC input terminal
Output circuit supply fuse blown
PLC program logic not reaching output — rung condition not met due to upstream logic fault
Field device fault — solenoid coil open circuit or relay coil fault
Output module power supply fault — 24VDC supply absent or low

checks: In PLC online monitoring, confirm the output address is TRUE in the program
Check the output module LED for the channel in question
Measure voltage at the output terminal with a multimeter
Check output module power supply and fuses
Check field wiring continuity from output terminal to field device
Check the field device independently — measure coil resistance of solenoid or relay
safety_precautions: Measure voltages carefully — output terminals carry live voltage when energised
Isolation required before working on field wiring terminals
Do not use a short circuit to test output module operation — this will damage the output channel
Confirm machine is in a safe state before forcing outputs in the PLC — forcing outputs can cause unexpected machine movement
faults.escalation: Escalate if output module requires replacement. Escalate if program logic fault is identified — requires controls engineer review.
faults.documentation_notes: Record output address, module slot, channel number, voltage measured, and wiring continuity result. Record any fuses replaced.
faults.gmp_notes: Output module replacement in GMP environments should be documented. Run a functional test after replacement.
preventive_actions: Check output module terminal tightness at planned maintenance intervals
Use individual channel fuses on high-value or high-risk output circuits
reliability_improvements: Use output modules with per-channel diagnostics to reduce fault-finding time
Consider IO-Link for smart device connectivity with remote diagnostics

faults.title: Safety Relay Fault or Non-Reset
symptoms: Machine not starting after E-stop reset
Safety relay not resetting when reset button pressed
Safety relay LED showing fault or monitoring fault
Machine stopping unexpectedly — safety relay dropping out during run
Safety relay input circuit LED not illuminated
faults.meaning: The safety relay has detected an open circuit in one of its monitored safety input channels, or the reset sequence is not being completed correctly. Safety relays are designed to fail safe — they will not reset unless all monitored inputs are correct and the reset sequence is properly completed. Do not attempt to bypass the safety relay.

causes.cause: E-stop button or safety switch contact open — device activated or contact failed
Safety relay reset sequence not completed correctly
Wiring fault in safety input circuit — broken wire or loose connection causing open circuit
Safety relay cross-fault detected — short circuit between the two input channels
Safety relay output contacts worn
Safety relay module fault — internal relay fault

checks: Check all E-stop buttons and safety switches in the monitored circuit — confirm all are released and in the correct state
Check safety relay status LEDs — most safety relays have LEDs indicating which input channel is open
Attempt the reset sequence as per the machine documentation
With safety relay power on and all E-stops released, measure voltage on each input channel at the safety relay terminals
Check safety circuit wiring for broken wires or loose terminals
Check for cross-connections between Channel 1 and Channel 2 wiring
safety_precautions: Never bypass, bridge, or disable a safety relay — this removes a critical safety protection function
Any modification to the safety circuit must be performed by a competent person and must be risk-assessed in accordance with ISO 13849 and ISO 12100 principles
Safety relay replacement or circuit modification must be followed by functional testing of all safety functions before returning to service
faults.escalation: Escalate to a competent engineer for all safety relay and safety circuit fault investigation. Safety circuit work must not be carried out by personnel without appropriate competence. Any change to a safety function must be reviewed and documented.
faults.documentation_notes: Record safety relay type, status LEDs observed, input channel voltages measured, and the specific safety device found to be causing the open circuit. Record corrective action and functional test result.
faults.gmp_notes: Safety circuit modifications or repairs in GMP environments may require change control and re-verification of the safety function. The safety function performance level must not be compromised.
preventive_actions: Include E-stop button and safety switch functional test in planned maintenance schedule
Check safety circuit wiring and terminal tightness at each planned maintenance visit
Perform periodic safety function test — confirm machine stops correctly when each E-stop and safety switch is activated
reliability_improvements: Use safety relay modules with diagnostics output to PLC for faster fault identification
Consider safety PLC for complex safety circuits to improve diagnostics and flexibility

faults.title: Emergency Stop Circuit Failure to Reset
symptoms: Machine will not start after E-stop is released
E-stop button appears to be released but circuit remains open
Safety relay or safety PLC showing E-stop input fault
Reset button has no effect
faults.meaning: The emergency stop circuit is not returning to a healthy state after the E-stop device is released. The E-stop circuit is a safety-critical function — the cause must be correctly identified and the circuit verified before restart.

causes.cause: E-stop button mechanism faulty — button released but internal contact not returning to closed state
E-stop button not fully released — twisted or jammed button body
Another E-stop in the circuit still activated — multi-E-stop circuits require all devices to be released
Wiring fault in E-stop circuit — broken wire causing permanent open circuit
E-stop contact contaminated or corroded — poor contact closure
E-stop cable damaged by mechanical interference

checks: Physically inspect every E-stop button and safety pull-cord on the machine — confirm each one is released and reset correctly
Check E-stop button mushroom head — rotate and pull to confirm it has returned to released position
Measure continuity across each E-stop device contact with a multimeter (circuit isolated)
Check safety relay or safety PLC input LEDs to identify which channel is open
Inspect E-stop cable runs for damage
Check E-stop terminal connections — confirm wiring is secure and correct
components: Safety relay or safety PLC input module
safety_precautions: Never bypass the E-stop circuit under any circumstances — this is a fundamental safety requirement
E-stop circuit repair must be performed by a competent person
After any repair, the E-stop function must be fully tested — confirm machine stops when each E-stop device is activated
Document all E-stop circuit work and functional test results
faults.escalation: Escalate to a competent engineer for E-stop circuit fault diagnosis and repair. E-stop button replacement requires functional verification testing before return to service.
faults.documentation_notes: Record which E-stop device was found faulty, the fault found, corrective action, and functional test result. This is a safety-critical record.
faults.gmp_notes: E-stop circuit repair in GMP environments must be documented. Functional test evidence must be retained.
preventive_actions: Include E-stop functional test in weekly or monthly planned maintenance schedule
Inspect E-stop button condition and cable routing at each planned maintenance visit
reliability_improvements: Use E-stop buttons with positive-opening contacts (as required by IEC 60947-5-5) to ensure reliable contact opening
Consider E-stop circuit with cross-monitoring (dual channel) for higher safety integrity applications

faults.title: Reject Station Not Ejecting Faulty Product
symptoms: Faulty or out-of-spec product passing through reject station without being ejected
Reject confirmation sensor not detecting product in reject bin
PLC reject output activating but reject mechanism not operating
Reject air blast or pusher not firing at correct time
Machine not counting reject events
faults.meaning: The reject station is failing to remove products that have been flagged for rejection. In food, pharmaceutical, or safety-critical manufacturing, faulty product reaching downstream processes or customers is a serious non-conformance.

causes.cause: Timing error — reject output firing too early or too late relative to product arrival
Reject mechanism fault — air blast solenoid not firing, pusher cylinder not extending
Product speed variation — product arriving at reject station outside the timing window
Reject air blast pressure low — insufficient force to deflect product
Reject confirmation sensor fault
PLC reject logic fault — reject signal not being passed to the reject output correctly

checks: Run a controlled test with a known reject product — observe reject station behaviour with PLC online monitoring active
Check PLC reject output timing against product tracking
Check reject mechanism operation independently — fire the reject output manually and confirm mechanism operates
Check air pressure at reject air blast nozzle
Check reject confirmation sensor operation
Check product tracking — confirm line speed has not changed
Review PLC reject logic
components: Reject mechanism (air blast, pusher, or flap)
safety_precautions: Reject station testing with known faulty product must be conducted as a controlled test — record the test and its result
Ensure guarding is in place around reject mechanisms before running
Do not use real product from the production stream for reject station testing without authorisation
faults.escalation: Escalate immediately if reject station failure results in known faulty product leaving the machine — this is a potential product recall situation requiring QA escalation. Escalate controls engineer if PLC timing or logic fault is suspected.
faults.documentation_notes: Record the test conducted, the result, the fault found, and the corrective action. If faulty product has reached downstream, document quantity and batch details and notify QA immediately.
faults.gmp_notes: Reject station failure in food, pharmaceutical, or medical device manufacturing is a critical quality event. A reject station failure during production may require a batch hold and investigation before product release.
preventive_actions: Include reject station functional test (with test piece) in shift start checks
Check reject mechanism operation and reject sensor function at each planned maintenance visit
Verify reject timing parameters after any line speed change
reliability_improvements: Install reject confirmation monitoring with machine stop if product is not confirmed rejected after a reject command
Consider dedicated reject verification vision system for critical reject applications

faults.title: Product Jam on Conveyor or Transfer Point
symptoms: Machine stopped on jam detection alarm
Product bunched up at a specific point on the line
Product damaged or deformed at jam location
Anti-jam sensor or pressure sensor triggered
Machine refusing to restart after jam cleared
faults.meaning: Product has accumulated and blocked at a point in the production or transfer path. Jams damage product, can damage machinery, and cause downtime. Recurring jams at the same location indicate an underlying mechanical or process cause that must be addressed.

causes.cause: Downstream machine or process stopped or slowed, causing product to accumulate
Transfer gap, guide, or rail set incorrectly for the product size or format
Product defect — misshapen or incorrectly formed product causing a blockage
Conveyor belt worn or damaged — surface condition causing product to catch
Guide rail or transfer plate worn, damaged, or misaligned
Line speed imbalance between upstream and downstream sections

checks: Isolate and lock out before clearing any jam — do not reach into running machinery
Identify the exact location where jam originated — not just where product has built up
Check for product damage or defects at the jam origin point
Check guide rail settings and transfer gap dimensions
Check downstream machine status
Check conveyor belt surface condition at the jam location
Review jam history — confirm if this is a recurring jam at the same location
safety_precautions: Never reach into a running conveyor or transfer mechanism to clear a jam
Full isolation and LOTO required before manually clearing a product jam from inside guarded zones
Beware of stored product pressure behind a jam — product can move rapidly when jam is cleared
Where heated or pressurised product is involved, additional PPE may be required
faults.escalation: Escalate if jam is recurring — mechanical investigation and adjustment required. Escalate if product damage is significant and batch integrity is in question. Escalate to QA if damaged product may have entered the product stream.
faults.documentation_notes: Record jam location, product condition at jam, and any mechanical findings. Record number of jams in the shift — trend data is essential for identifying recurring causes.
faults.gmp_notes: In food and pharmaceutical environments, product damaged in a jam may be contaminated or compromised. Do not return jam product to the production stream without QA authorisation.
preventive_actions: Check and adjust guide rail settings at each format change
Include transfer point and guide rail condition check in planned maintenance schedule
Monitor jam frequency by location to prioritise mechanical improvements
reliability_improvements: Install jam detection sensors at all known jam-prone points with automatic machine stop
Consider conveyor accumulation zones upstream of known bottlenecks

faults.title: Intermittent Machine Stop Without Fault Code
symptoms: Machine stopping randomly without a clear fault message
Machine restarting normally after stop — no fault found
Fault log showing no recorded fault at time of stop
Stops occurring more frequently over time
Stops correlated with specific times, environmental conditions, or production rates
faults.meaning: The machine is stopping for a reason that is not being captured by the fault logging system. Intermittent stops are among the most difficult faults to diagnose — they require systematic data collection over time rather than a single inspection. Do not assume the machine is healthy just because it restarts without a fault.

causes.cause: Intermittent wiring fault — loose connection causing momentary signal loss on a critical input
Power supply voltage instability — supply dipping momentarily causing PLC or drive fault that self-clears
Sensor signal instability — sensor triggering incorrectly and causing a PLC program branch that stops the machine
Thermal issue — component overheating intermittently and recovering after cooling
EMC interference — electrical noise causing spurious PLC inputs or communication errors
PLC program fault — logic condition causing a stop in specific product or process states
Compressed air pressure drops momentarily

checks: Enable PLC fault logging if not already active — capture all input state changes and program events at time of next stop
Check PLC and VFD fault histories for any fault codes recorded at the time of stops
Check all control panel wiring and terminal connections — look for loose, corroded, or damaged terminations
Check 24VDC control supply voltage stability
Check compressed air pressure log or fit a data-logging pressure gauge
Check for patterns — note time of day, ambient temperature, production rate, or machine state at time of each stop
Check for EMC sources near the control cabinet
safety_precautions: Do not disable safety functions to try to identify the fault — work within the machine's safety architecture
If stops are becoming more frequent, the underlying fault is likely developing — plan a controlled investigation
Do not bypass interlocks to investigate unexplained stops
faults.escalation: Escalate to controls engineer if PLC program review is required. Escalate to electrical engineer if power supply or EMC investigation is needed. Do not allow intermittent stops to continue indefinitely — the underlying fault will typically worsen.
faults.documentation_notes: Record every stop — time, machine state, production rate, and any observations. Patterns in this data are usually the key to finding the root cause.
faults.gmp_notes: In GMP environments, unexplained machine stops must be investigated and documented. If stops occur during validated production runs, this may be a process deviation requiring QA notification.
preventive_actions: Ensure PLC fault logging is enabled and capturing sufficient detail
Include control panel terminal tightness check in planned maintenance schedule
Check 24VDC supply voltage at planned maintenance visits
reliability_improvements: Install power quality monitoring on machine control supply to detect voltage dips
Use PLC data logging or OEE system to record all stops with timestamps for trend analysis

faults.title: PLC Digital Input Not Reading Field Signal
symptoms: PLC input shown as OFF in program when field device should be ON
Machine sequence waiting for input that is not arriving
Input indicator LED on I/O module not illuminating when field device is active
Input LED illuminated continuously — input stuck ON
faults.meaning: The PLC is not receiving the correct signal from the field device on the input channel. This can halt a machine sequence or cause incorrect machine behaviour.

causes.cause: Field device fault — sensor, switch, or limit switch not changing state
Wiring fault — broken wire or loose terminal between field device and PLC input terminal
Field device power supply fault — sensor not powered
PLC input module channel fault — input channel not reading correctly
Signal voltage too low — voltage drop on long cable run
Incorrect wiring polarity — NPN sensor connected to PNP input or vice versa

checks: Check field device indicator (sensor LED or switch indicator) — confirm device is operating
Measure signal voltage at the PLC input terminal when the field device is active
Check field device power supply voltage
Check wiring continuity from field device to PLC terminal
Substitute a known good sensor
If signal is present at terminal but PLC input is not reading, suspect input module channel fault
Check wiring polarity for PNP/NPN sensor types
safety_precautions: Measure live voltages with appropriate CAT-rated meter and probes
Confirm machine is in a safe state before forcing inputs or substituting sensors
Where the input is part of a safety function, escalate to a competent engineer — do not bypass
faults.escalation: Escalate if PLC input module requires replacement. Escalate if wiring fault requires significant cable replacement.
faults.documentation_notes: Record input address, terminal number, voltage measured, and fault found. Record replacement part number if sensor replaced.
faults.gmp_notes: Sensor replacement in GMP environments should be documented. Run a functional test to confirm input operation after replacement.
preventive_actions: Include input terminal tightness check in planned maintenance
Check sensor cable condition at each planned maintenance visit
reliability_improvements: Use IO-Link sensors for remote diagnostics and signal quality monitoring
Use cable drag chain cable specifically rated for continuous flexing in moving cable applications

faults.title: Guard Door Interlock Not Resetting
symptoms: Machine will not start after guard door is closed
Safety relay or safety PLC showing guard interlock input open
Guard door appears closed but interlock switch not making
Guard interlock LED not illuminating when door is closed
faults.meaning: The guard door interlock switch is not confirming that the guard is closed. The safety system is correctly preventing machine start. This may be mechanical (switch not actuating), electrical (wiring or switch fault), or misalignment between the actuator and the switch.

causes.cause: Guard door actuator (key or tongue) misaligned with switch body — door closing but actuator not entering switch
Interlock switch fault — switch contacts failed or switch damaged
Guard door sagging or distorted — door no longer aligns correctly with switch
Wiring fault — broken wire or loose connection on interlock switch circuit
Interlock switch mounting loose — switch has moved out of position

checks: Visually inspect guard door closure — confirm door is fully closed and latched
Inspect interlock switch actuator alignment — confirm actuator enters switch correctly when door closes
Check switch mounting — confirm switch is secure and has not moved
Measure continuity across switch contacts (circuit isolated) when actuator is inserted
Check wiring and connector at switch
Check guard door hinges and frame — confirm door has not sagged
components: Guard door actuator (tongue, key, or magnet)
Safety relay or safety PLC input
safety_precautions: Never defeat, tape, or bypass a guard door interlock switch — this removes a physical safety barrier protection
Interlock switch replacement must be followed by a full functional test — confirm machine does not start when guard is open
Use only positive-opening contact switches for guard interlocks in line with IEC 60947-5-1 principles
Guard door interlock work must be performed by a competent person and documented
faults.escalation: Escalate to a competent engineer for guard interlock repair and functional verification. Escalate if guard door structure requires repair.
faults.documentation_notes: Record switch type and part number, fault found, corrective action, and functional test result (machine does not start with guard open).
faults.gmp_notes: Guard interlock repair and functional testing must be documented in GMP environments. Interlock function should be included in equipment validation.
preventive_actions: Include guard door interlock functional test in planned maintenance schedule
Check interlock switch mounting and actuator alignment at each planned maintenance visit
reliability_improvements: Use coded magnetic safety switches on guard doors in high-vibration or washdown environments
Install guard door monitoring with PLC alarm to detect interlock approaching end of life

faults.title: Packaging Machine Film Tension Fault
symptoms: Film tension alarm on HMI
Film tearing or breaking during operation
Film loosely wrapped or not correctly tensioned around product
Film dancer arm at end of travel
Film drive servo or motor showing overload or torque fault
faults.meaning: The packaging film is not being fed and tensioned correctly. This results in poor packaging quality, broken film causing stoppage, or product not correctly sealed. The tension system must maintain consistent film tension throughout the reel cycle.

causes.cause: Film reel brake or tension control not functioning — reel overfeeding or underfeeding film
Film splice poorly made — film break at splice point
Film type changed without adjusting tension settings
Film dancer arm or tension roller stiff or seized
Film reel core not correctly fitted — reel not running concentrically
Film drive nip roller worn — not gripping film consistently

checks: Check film tension setting and dancer arm position
Inspect film reel — confirm it is correctly seated on the mandrel and running concentrically
Check film brake or tension control mechanism
Inspect film splice — confirm it is correctly made and film is not tearing at splice
Check dancer arm movement — confirm arm is free to move throughout its range
Check nip roller condition
Confirm film specification matches machine tension settings
components: Film tension sensor or dancer position sensor
safety_precautions: Rotating film rolls and nip rollers are entrapment hazards — machine must be isolated before threading film or adjusting rollers
Hot sealing jaws present a burn hazard — confirm jaws are cooled before reaching into the film path
Film edges can cause cuts — use appropriate hand protection
faults.escalation: Escalate if dancer arm or tension control mechanism requires mechanical repair. Escalate if film type change requires a formal settings change and validation.
faults.documentation_notes: Record film specification, tension setting, and fault type. Record any splice failure location.
faults.gmp_notes: In pharmaceutical or food packaging, film specification changes require formal change control. Film tension affects seal quality — assess seal integrity if tension was out of specification during production.
preventive_actions: Include dancer arm, tension roller, and nip roller inspection in planned maintenance schedule
Document approved tension settings for each film specification used on the machine
reliability_improvements: Install automatic tension control with closed-loop feedback for consistent tension regardless of reel diameter
Use film break detection sensor to stop machine immediately on film break

faults.title: Heat Seal Fault — Poor or Incomplete Seal
symptoms: Seal integrity alarm on machine or seal test equipment
Seals visually incomplete, weak, or delaminated
Product leaking through seal after packaging
Seal jaw temperature alarm
Seal strength test failures on production samples
faults.meaning: The heat seal is not achieving the required bond strength or integrity. In food and pharmaceutical packaging, seal integrity is a critical quality and safety parameter. A failed seal can allow product contamination, spoilage, or leakage.

causes.cause: Seal jaw temperature too low — insufficient heat to melt and bond the film seal layer
Seal jaw pressure insufficient — jaws not closing with enough force to form bond
Seal dwell time too short — insufficient contact time at temperature for bond to form
Film contamination at seal zone — product, oil, or moisture preventing film-to-film bonding
Seal jaw surface worn or damaged — uneven pressure across seal width
Seal jaw PTFE coating worn — film sticking to jaw and tearing on opening
Film specification changed — different seal layer requiring different temperature or dwell

checks: Check seal jaw temperature against specification — measure with calibrated thermocouple
Check seal jaw pressure — confirm pneumatic pressure to jaw cylinder is correct
Check seal dwell time setting in machine parameters
Inspect seal jaw surface — check for wear, damage, or PTFE coating condition
Check product at seal zone — confirm no product contamination in the seal area
Run seal integrity test on recent packs
Confirm film specification has not changed
safety_precautions: Seal jaws operate at high temperatures (typically 100-250°C) — severe burn hazard
Always confirm jaws have cooled to a safe temperature before inspection or adjustment
Isolation required before adjusting seal jaw pressure or dwell time
Hot seal jaw surfaces can ignite packaging materials if machine is stopped with jaws closed on film
faults.escalation: Escalate to QA if seal integrity failures have occurred during production — batch assessment required. Escalate if seal jaw replacement or thermocouple replacement is required.
faults.documentation_notes: Record seal temperature found, jaw pressure found, dwell time setting, and seal integrity test result. In GMP environments, this is a quality event and must be documented formally.
faults.gmp_notes: Seal integrity is a critical quality parameter in food and pharmaceutical packaging. Seal faults during production must be reported to QA. Seal jaw temperature, pressure, and dwell time are typically validated parameters requiring change control.
preventive_actions: Include seal jaw thermocouple calibration check in planned maintenance schedule
Inspect seal jaw surface and PTFE coating at each planned maintenance visit
Run seal integrity test at start of shift and after format change
reliability_improvements: Install seal jaw temperature data logging to detect gradual drift
Consider vision-based seal inspection system to detect seal faults 100% inline

faults.title: Labeller Not Applying Label Correctly
symptoms: Labels applied skewed, off-position, or folded
Labels not adhering to product surface
Label missing from product
Label feed alarm on HMI
Label registration mark sensor not triggering correctly
faults.meaning: The label applicator is not placing labels correctly on the product. In regulated manufacturing, label placement and content are critical — mislabelled or unlabelled product is a serious quality and regulatory non-conformance.

causes.cause: Label registration not set correctly — label position on reel out of sync with applicator cycle
Label reel running out or label web break
Product surface unsuitable — wet, dusty, cold, or contaminated surface preventing adhesion
Applicator pad or tamp mechanism misaligned
Label feed drive fault — labels not advancing correctly
Label registration sensor contaminated or misaligned
Label specification changed without adjusting machine settings

checks: Check label reel — confirm labels are present and web is not broken
Check label registration sensor — clean lens and confirm alignment with registration mark on label web
Check registration setting — run label advance test and confirm position
Inspect applicator pad or tamp mechanism alignment
Check product surface condition at labelling zone
Check label feed drive — confirm liner is being taken up correctly
Confirm label specification matches current machine settings
components: Label feed drive and nip rollers
safety_precautions: Rotating label reel and nip rollers are entrapment hazards — isolate before threading label web
Compressed air used in tamp applicators — vent before working on applicator mechanism
faults.escalation: Escalate immediately if mislabelled or unlabelled product may have passed the label check point — QA notification required.
faults.documentation_notes: Record label position found, fault identified, corrective action, and verification test result. If mislabelled product has occurred, document quantity, batch, and QA notification.
faults.gmp_notes: In pharmaceutical, food, and medical device manufacturing, labelling errors are among the most critical non-conformances. A labelling fault during production must be reported to QA immediately. Label parameters are typically validated requiring formal change control.
preventive_actions: Include label registration check and sensor cleaning in shift start checks
Inspect label feed drive and nip rollers at each planned maintenance visit
Implement label check (camera or label detect sensor) downstream of applicator
reliability_improvements: Install 100% label presence and position verification camera system
Use label reel low sensor with advance warning before reel runs out

faults.title: Checkweigher Giving Incorrect Weight Readings
symptoms: Checkweigher rejecting product within specification
Checkweigher passing product outside specification
Weight readings unstable or fluctuating
Checkweigher requiring frequent zero adjustment
Checkweigher alarm — static weight or overload fault
faults.meaning: The checkweigher is not giving accurate weight measurements. This is a critical quality and legal compliance issue — incorrect rejections waste product; incorrect passes allow out-of-spec product to reach customers.

causes.cause: Weighing cell contaminated — product, liquid, or debris on load cell affecting reading
Vibration from nearby machinery affecting weighing signal
Product speed too high or too low for checkweigher design
Load cell fault — load cell damaged, drifting, or at end of life
Checkweigher not zeroed or calibrated after format change
Draught or airflow across the weigh platform
Product not stable on weigh platform — product rocking or bouncing

checks: Clean weigh platform and load cell area — remove any product residue or debris
Zero the checkweigher and run a known weight verification (certified test weights)
Check for sources of vibration near the checkweigher
Check for draught or airflow across the weigh platform
Check product speed through the checkweigher
Run statistical weight check on recent packs
Check load cell signal using checkweigher diagnostic mode if available
safety_precautions: Use certified test weights for verification — do not use uncertified weights for a legal-for-trade checkweigher
Isolation required before cleaning underneath the weigh platform or accessing load cell connections
Do not place excessive weight on the weigh platform — load cell overload can cause permanent damage
faults.escalation: Escalate to calibration technician if load cell replacement or calibration is required. Escalate to QA if out-of-specification product may have passed. In legal-for-trade applications, escalate to the responsible person for weights and measures compliance.
faults.documentation_notes: Record the verification weight results (before and after adjustment). In GMP or legal-for-trade environments, calibration records must be retained.
faults.gmp_notes: Checkweigher accuracy is a validated parameter in pharmaceutical and food manufacturing. Any adjustment or repair requires documented calibration verification with traceable test weights.
preventive_actions: Include checkweigher zero check and test weight verification in shift start checks
Clean weigh platform at each shift change
Include load cell condition check in planned maintenance schedule
reliability_improvements: Install vibration isolation mounts on checkweigher frame if vibration is a recurring issue
Use remote checkweigher data monitoring to trend weight accuracy over time

faults.title: Metal Detector False Rejects or Missed Detection
symptoms: Metal detector rejecting good product (false reject)
Metal detector not detecting test piece (missed detection)
Metal detector sensitivity alarm
High reject rate with no metal contamination found in rejected product
Metal detector requiring frequent rebalancing
faults.meaning: The metal detector is not performing to its specified detection sensitivity. False rejects waste product; missed detections allow contaminated product to reach customers. In food and pharmaceutical manufacturing, metal detector performance is a critical food safety and quality control requirement.

causes.cause: Product effect — high moisture, salt, or conductive product causing signal interference
Vibration or mechanical disturbance affecting detector signal
Metallic components in product (foil, clips, etc.) being detected
Electromagnetic interference from nearby equipment — VFDs, motors, welders
Detector head aperture contaminated
Sensitivity set too high — beyond reliable detection capability for the product
Detector requiring rebalancing — head balance drifted due to temperature change or product buildup

checks: Run test piece verification — pass Fe, Non-Fe, and SS test pieces through at start, middle, and end of product flow
Check detector balance — rebalance if required as per manufacturer procedure
Check detector aperture — inspect for product buildup or contamination
Check for nearby sources of electromagnetic interference
Check for vibration sources near the detector
Review product specifications — confirm product characteristics have not changed
Check sensitivity setting against qualified sensitivity for this product
safety_precautions: Metal detector test piece verification must be conducted according to the documented procedure — test pieces must be certified and traceable
Do not adjust sensitivity settings without following the documented qualification procedure
If metal contamination is suspected in product, do not release product without QA authorisation
faults.escalation: Escalate immediately to QA if metal contamination is detected or if the detector has been operating outside its qualified sensitivity. Escalate to detector manufacturer if detector requires rebalancing or repair beyond site competence.
faults.documentation_notes: Record all test piece verification results. Record any sensitivity adjustments made, before and after sensitivity values, and authorisation for the change.
faults.gmp_notes: Metal detector performance is a Critical Control Point (CCP) in HACCP-based food safety systems. Test piece records must be retained as food safety evidence. A failure of the metal detector during production must trigger a batch hold and QA assessment.
preventive_actions: Conduct test piece verification at shift start, shift change, and after any product changeover
Clean detector aperture at each product changeover
Include detector service in annual planned maintenance schedule
reliability_improvements: Use metal detector with automatic product learn and product effect compensation
Install metal detector data logging to record all test piece results and reject events

faults.title: Pneumatic Cylinder Rod Seal Leak
symptoms: Air leaking from cylinder rod seal — audible or visible
Cylinder losing pressure and retracting under load
Oil mist or seal material around cylinder rod
Cylinder not maintaining position under load
Increased air consumption on the circuit
faults.meaning: The rod seal on the pneumatic cylinder has failed or is worn, allowing compressed air to escape past the rod. This reduces cylinder force, causes the cylinder to lose position under load, and wastes compressed air.

causes.cause: Rod seal worn — end of life after extended use
Cylinder rod surface damaged — scratched or corroded rod cutting seal lip
Cylinder side-loaded — misalignment causing uneven rod seal wear
Incorrect lubricant used — incompatible lubricant causing seal swelling or degradation
Seal material unsuitable for operating environment

checks: Isolate and vent pneumatic supply before inspecting cylinder rod seal
Inspect rod surface for scratches, corrosion, or damage
Check cylinder alignment — confirm rod is not side-loaded
Confirm seal material specification is appropriate for the operating environment
safety_precautions: Isolate and fully vent the pneumatic supply before any cylinder disassembly
Support the cylinder load before removing the cylinder — load must not be held by air pressure alone during maintenance
In GMP environments, oil mist from a leaking cylinder is a contamination risk — assess and notify QA if product may have been affected
faults.escalation: Escalate if cylinder rod surface is damaged — rod or cylinder replacement required. Escalate if cylinder requires a seal kit not held on site.
faults.documentation_notes: Record cylinder reference, seal condition found, rod condition found, and replacement action taken.
faults.gmp_notes: Lubricants used in pneumatic cylinders must be food-grade approved where there is any risk of contact with product or packaging. Cylinder seal replacement should be documented.
preventive_actions: Include cylinder rod seal condition check in planned maintenance schedule
Keep cylinder seal kits as site spares for all cylinder types in use
reliability_improvements: Use stainless steel rod cylinders in corrosive or washdown environments
Install cylinder alignment guides to reduce side loading on rod seals

faults.title: Conveyor Speed Variation or Inconsistent Speed
symptoms: Conveyor running at inconsistent speed — speeding up and slowing down
Product spacing on conveyor inconsistent
VFD output frequency fluctuating without command change
Encoder feedback fault on VFD or PLC
Line speed alarm on HMI
faults.meaning: The conveyor is not maintaining its commanded speed. This affects product spacing, timing, and downstream process coordination. In packaging and processing lines, consistent conveyor speed is critical for machine synchronisation.

causes.cause: VFD reference signal unstable — analogue speed reference signal affected by interference or loose connection
Mechanical slip — drive belt slipping or gearbox backlash causing speed variation
Encoder fault — encoder giving incorrect feedback to VFD or PLC
VFD acceleration/deceleration ramps set incorrectly — conveyor surging on speed change commands
Load variation — varying belt load causing speed variation on an open-loop VFD system

checks: Check VFD output frequency on VFD display — confirm it matches commanded speed
Check VFD speed reference signal — measure analogue input voltage or check fieldbus command
Check encoder wiring and connector — confirm encoder feedback is stable
Check drive belt or gearbox for slip or backlash
Check VFD acceleration and deceleration ramp settings
safety_precautions: Do not adjust VFD parameters on a running machine without understanding the effect on downstream processes
Isolation required before inspecting drive belt or gearbox
faults.escalation: Escalate to controls engineer if VFD speed reference or encoder fault is suspected. Escalate if gearbox repair is required.
faults.documentation_notes: Record commanded speed, actual speed measured, VFD output frequency, and any wiring or mechanical findings.
faults.gmp_notes: Conveyor speed is often a validated parameter in pharmaceutical and food manufacturing lines. Any speed adjustment must be within validated limits and documented.
preventive_actions: Include encoder condition check in planned maintenance schedule
Check drive belt tension and condition at each planned maintenance visit
reliability_improvements: Use closed-loop VFD with encoder feedback for speed-critical conveyor applications
Use shielded cable for analogue speed reference signals to reduce interference

faults.title: Product Orientation Fault on Infeed
symptoms: Products arriving at machine in incorrect orientation
Orientation sensor detecting fault — machine stopping or rejecting
Products tumbling, falling, or rotating on infeed conveyor
High reject rate at orientation check station
faults.meaning: Products are not arriving at the machine inlet in the required orientation. Incorrect orientation causes machine jams, packaging faults, and high reject rates.

causes.cause: Infeed conveyor guide rails set incorrectly for product dimensions or format
Product inherently unstable — product shape or centre of gravity making it prone to tipping
Infeed conveyor speed too high — products tumbling at transfer points
Transfer point gap or step causing products to catch or rotate
Upstream machine not consistently orientating product before infeed

checks: Observe product flow on infeed — identify exactly where orientation fault occurs
Check guide rail settings for current product format
