Check infeed speed — reduce if products are tumbling at transfers
Inspect transfer points — check for gaps, steps, or edges causing orientation loss
Check product consistency — confirm product dimensions and shape are within specification
safety_precautions: Do not adjust guide rails or transfer points with machine running and guarding open
Isolation required before reaching into the infeed zone
faults.escalation: Escalate if product design is causing fundamental orientation instability — requires product or packaging design review. Escalate if format change documentation needs to be updated.
faults.documentation_notes: Record guide rail settings and transfer point adjustments made. Record the product format and any product dimension variations found.
faults.gmp_notes: In GMP environments, guide rail settings for each product format should be documented in the format changeover procedure.
preventive_actions: Document approved guide rail settings for each product format
Include infeed guide rail check in format changeover procedure
reliability_improvements: Install product orientation detection with automatic reject before the main machine infeed
Consider vibratory or belt orientator for inherently unstable products

faults.title: Machine Not Starting — Multiple Interlock Conditions Not Met
symptoms: Machine will not start when start button is pressed
HMI showing multiple interlock or pre-condition faults
No single obvious fault — machine in a general not-ready state
Start button press acknowledged but machine does not run
faults.meaning: The machine start permissive conditions are not all met. Modern machines require a list of conditions to be satisfied before start is permitted. This is a normal safety and operational protection feature — the challenge is identifying which condition is not met.

causes.cause: Guard door or safety interlock not reset — safety circuit not healthy
E-stop not reset — E-stop circuit not cleared
Machine not in correct mode — local/remote, auto/manual, or mode selector in wrong position
Fault not cleared — a previous fault has not been acknowledged and reset
Services not available — compressed air, utilities, or upstream machine not ready
PLC start permissive logic fault — a permissive condition is incorrectly not clearing

checks: Check HMI fault list — note every active fault or interlock message
Check all E-stop buttons are released and reset
Check all guard doors are closed and interlocked
Check machine mode selector switches
Check compressed air supply pressure
Check upstream and downstream machine status
Check PLC start permissive status in online monitoring — identify which permissive is not clearing
safety_precautions: Do not attempt to force a machine start by bypassing interlock conditions — the conditions exist for safety and process reasons
Systematically work through the HMI fault list rather than guessing
faults.escalation: Escalate to controls engineer if a PLC permissive condition is not clearing despite all physical conditions being correct.
faults.documentation_notes: Record all HMI fault messages present and the sequence of actions taken to clear them.
faults.gmp_notes: In GMP environments, machine start condition records can be important for batch record documentation.
preventive_actions: Ensure HMI fault messages clearly describe the specific condition not met
Train operators on machine start sequence and common pre-start conditions
reliability_improvements: Implement HMI start permissive status screen showing all conditions and their current state to speed up fault finding
Log all start attempts and failure reasons in the PLC for trend analysis

faults.title: Compressed Air FRL Unit Maintenance Required
symptoms: Low air pressure alarm despite adequate site supply pressure
Water or contamination in pneumatic circuit downstream of FRL
FRL filter bowl full of water or contamination
Lubricator bowl empty — no oil mist in circuit
Regulator output pressure unstable or not holding set pressure
faults.meaning: The Filter-Regulator-Lubricator (FRL) unit requires maintenance. The FRL is the first line of protection for the pneumatic circuit — a poorly maintained FRL allows water, contamination, and pressure variation into the circuit, causing premature wear of valves, cylinders, and seals throughout the machine.

causes.cause: Filter bowl full of water — auto-drain not functioning or drain interval too long
Filter element blocked — not replaced on schedule, causing pressure drop
Lubricator oil level low or empty — insufficient lubrication to downstream components
Regulator diaphragm or seat worn — regulator not maintaining set pressure
Auto-drain solenoid fault — condensate not being expelled automatically

checks: Check filter bowl — drain manually if water or contamination is present
Check filter element condition — replace if blocked or on schedule
Check lubricator oil level — refill with correct grade oil if low
Check regulator output pressure against set pressure
Check auto-drain function if fitted — confirm it is operating
safety_precautions: Vent downstream pneumatic circuit before removing filter element or lubricator bowl
Use only the correct lubricant grade for the FRL — incorrect oil can damage seals throughout the circuit
In food and pharmaceutical environments, use only approved food-grade lubricant in the lubricator
faults.escalation: Escalate if regulator requires replacement. Escalate if auto-drain solenoid requires replacement.
faults.documentation_notes: Record filter element replacement date, lubricator oil refill, and regulator pressure setting verified.
faults.gmp_notes: In food and pharmaceutical manufacturing, the lubricant used in the FRL must be food-grade approved (e.g. NSF H1 rated) where there is any risk of contact with product or packaging. Record lubricant type and batch in maintenance records.
preventive_actions: Include FRL drain, filter element inspection, and lubricator level check in weekly planned maintenance
Replace filter elements on a time or pressure differential schedule
Check and record regulator output pressure at each planned maintenance visit
reliability_improvements: Install automatic drain with timed solenoid to prevent water accumulation in filter bowl
Install differential pressure indicator on filter to give visual warning when element replacement is due
Consider point-of-use filters at critical pneumatic circuits in addition to the main FRL
